column1: How The Program Generator Should Read Each Column

column2: What it does
Stable primary key. Never reuse or renumber. Log sets against this, not the exercise name.
Top level filter. Used to hit weekly volume targets per muscle.
Ensures a program covers push, pull, squat, hinge, lunge, carry and core rather than repeating one pattern.
Compound movements are placed first in a session. Isolation movements fill remaining volume.
Hard filter set at onboarding. Bodyweight is a strict subset of Basic Gym, which is a strict subset of Full Gym.
Gate against user experience. A first time lifter is never served an Advanced movement in week one.
Flag for imbalance correction and for low equipment users who need more stimulus from light loads.
If No, progress via reps, tempo or leverage instead of adding weight.
Weighting, not a hard filter. A hypertrophy user can still be given a Strength tagged lift.

column1: Why It Is In The Database
column2: Selection rationale. Each exercise earns its slot by covering something no other exercise covers.
One line shown under the video on the exercise screen. Sells the exercise to the user in plain language.
The numbered cues shown on the exercise screen. Never more than three. Written as instructions, not theory.
The single most common error. Rendered in the grey callout box under the steps.
Ordered list. Position 1 is the closest match the app can serve. The swap button walks this list top down, skipping anything the user's equipment tier or skill level blocks.
Sanity check. Should never be zero. Anything under 3 is a coverage risk.
The closest to failure this exercise may ever be taken, for any user and any goal. The generator raises the grid RIR to meet this number, never lowers it.
Shown to the user on the exercise screen and used by the generator when a set is flagged.
Flat lookup. Key is Goal|Class. Every cell is a number or empty. Read the row, clamp it against Safety Rules, then write the set.
Applied after the grid lookup, in rule_id order. HARD rules block the session. SOFT rules warn and adjust.
The source of truth for swaps, keyed by ID so renames never break relationships. The name based columns on the other sheets are human readable views of this data.

column1: Substitution IDs / Failure Allowed / Spotter Recommended
column2: Substitution IDs mirror the name list by ID. Failure Allowed = No forces the Min RIR floor. Spotter Recommended triggers rule S09 for users training alone.
Which weekly structures exist and which one a given user gets. Selector matches days, then experience, then goal, first row wins.
The slot recipe for each day type. Filters map onto Exercise Database columns of the same name.
Weekly hard set budget per muscle, with the focal point adjustment and the 20 set cap.
The thirteen step order of operations. Anything the AI does when prescribing must trace back to a step on this sheet.
Screening maps to exactly one of four outcomes. The app never softens a program as a substitute for professional clearance.
Fixed weekly activities count against the weekly volume and conditioning budget. The AI classifies free text into a commitment_type and applies the row's adjustment.
Day types are placed onto real weekdays: rest floor, 48 hour spacing, commitment adjacency, missed session handling, exam mode. Rest days are calculated, never assumed.
Any user built structure compiles to a day_structure string, is validated (coverage, spacing, shared volume), and flows through the rest of the pipeline with zero special cases.

Structured equipment. Tags are comma separated, a slash inside a tag means any one of those satisfies it (bench/chair/bed). Onboarding collects the user's exact tags, the generator filters on them. Equipment Tier remains the coarse filter, tags are the precise one.
Impact: Low, Moderate or High landing forces. Supported: torso externally supported or ground based. These are what MODIFY_AND_CONTINUE screening modifications filter on.
Active = No removes an exercise from selection without deleting its history. video_url is the placeholder for the Form & video button content.
Conditioning is a first class day type with its own template, so Interval prescriptions are placed deterministically instead of floating.
The Firestore collections for dynamic user state: programs, workout instances, set logs, progression, swaps, flags. The static workbook plus these collections is the complete backend.
Template slots are a base, not the budget. The seven reconciliation rules close the gap between slot counts and weekly targets in a fixed order.

Maps every onboarding wizard field to the backend field and canonical value it produces. 'ask' fields are collected in the wizard, 'infer_ok' fields may be defaulted and refined later. The screening block gates generation under S05.
Runtime playbook for user-initiated exercise changes. Resolves every swap through the Substitutions list, re-prescribes and re-clamps safety. Distinguishes a swap from an S06 stop-symptom escalation.
Runtime playbook for adjusting reps, sets and load from logged performance. Bounded by the grid ranges and Safety Rules; progresses only when safe, pauses on fatigue, reverses on missed reps.
Runtime playbook for a mid-program goal change. Re-runs goal-dependent steps, preserves loads and history, eases intensity over one week, versions the program.

column1: Measurement Type / Load Unit / Stress Regions
column2: Measurement Type says how an exercise is logged and progressed (weight_reps, reps, duration, interval, assisted). Load Unit is its unit. Stress Regions are the joints/areas loaded, matched against users.affected_regions for injury-aware swaps (SW02).
Age (from date_of_birth, never defaulted) chooses the screening pathway. Under-18s use the young-person path with guardian consent; under-16 is blocked.
A Yes on the joint screening question triggers follow-ups that decide MODIFY vs professional clearance. A bare yes/no cannot route safely.
Progression rounds to the smallest achievable increment for the equipment type, not a fixed 2.5/5 kg.

Deterministic rules for the MODIFY_AND_CONTINUE screening branch. One row per flaggable region (lower_back, knee, shoulder, wrist, hip, ankle) giving exclude_ids, downgrade_ids, preferred alternatives, an RIR bump and a review period. Turns 'the AI avoids the injured area' into a fixed lookup the AI reads.

The governing instruction set for the coach AI: its job, hard nevers, the order it consults other sheets, out-of-scope refusals, and the fallback for unlisted requests. The app's extraInstructions field points here.
Deterministic lookup from a fuzzy structural request (day count + what to emphasise/de-emphasise) to a specific day_structure, so 'legs once a week' always resolves the same way.

Lets a user declare a future date range and pick how training adapts (full pause, maintenance, just-walk, fewer days, deload, or no change). Proactive twin of the missed-session rules. Protects progress; nothing below full_pause loses strength.
The implementation companion: what Claude Code builds (data model, screen, generator check, scheduler exemption) and what changes on-platform to ship the feature.

Every implementation task from across the workbook in one ordered, prioritised checklist (P0 safe-launch, P1 core engine, P2 enhancement), each pointing to its spec sheet and an acceptance check. The build plan for the repo.

In-app engineering notes (editable training days, catch-up on misses, pulling activity into the generator, optional activity step, injury region mapping, free-text red-flag scan). Fold into the Build Backlog.
